problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem C (data): analyze a year of Twitter-reported Wordle results (2022). Four required tasks: (1) build a model explaining variation and give a prediction interval for the number of reported results on 2023-03-01, plus test whether word attributes affect the hard-mode percentage; (2) for a given word/date predict the percentage distribution of (1,2,3,4,5,6,X) tries and quantify uncertainty, applied to the word "EERIE"; (3) classify words by difficulty and apply to "EERIE"; (4) report other interesting dataset findings. Team #2311035 solved it with three coupled models (LSTM, seven XGBoost regressors, RBF-SVM) plus a custom difficulty metric, packaged as a letter to the NYT puzzle editor.
models_used: Model I — LSTM (single-layer, 5 hidden units, look-back=33) for time-series forecast of daily reported-result count, implemented in PyTorch
Recursive/iterative multi-step forecasting — fundamental LSTM gives one-step output, so they recursively fed the prediction back in 60 times to reach the 2023-03-01 horizon
Monte-Carlo-style ensemble of 150 independent LSTM retrainings to build a 95% prediction interval [20745.72, 22914.74] via mean ± 1.96·sd over runs
OLS linear regression of hard-mode ratio on 4 word attributes (F-test, t-tests) to show no significant word effect on hard-mode percentage
Model II — seven separate XGBoost regressors, one per outcome class (1..6 tries and X), to predict the full percentage distribution for a given word; R²=0.68
Model III — Support Vector Machine with RBF kernel for 3-class word-difficulty classification (easy/medium/hard), accuracy 0.6556, F1 0.6634

Custom difficulty metric E_w — an unequally-weighted average of the try-distribution (weights 1,3,5,6,7,9,11 for 1..6 tries and X) used both as the label generator for Model III and as a cross-check on Model II
key_techniques: Two-stage normalization for skewed counts: natural-log transform first (data ~ gamma-distributed; plain Min-Max wouldn't separate values because changes were tiny as a fraction of the max), then Min-Max — explicitly justified rather than applied blindly
Cross-validation to tune the look-back window (settled on 33 days) and the number of LSTM units (5), kept tiny to avoid overfitting on only 359 days of data

Feature engineering of word attributes from external sources: daily-frequency rank (Leipzig Corpora 2022), initial-letter-is-vowel binary, consecutive-letter binary, average letter frequency (Concise Oxford Dictionary), plus decision-tree 'depth' (Poirrier/Selby optimal-strategy trees) and max repeated-letter count (charNum)
Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) to reduce 6 hand-built features to 3 dominant ones (daily frequency, charNum, letter frequency)
RobustScaler on frequency-rank feature to blunt extreme outliers (huge rank values)

Per-class regressor decomposition: rather than one multi-output model, train 7 independent XGBoost regressors and then audit that predicted percentages sum to ~100% (showed sum in (98,102), variance 0.71) as an internal consistency / uncertainty check
Sensitivity analysis using near-twin words (Royal/Sport/Prove, attributes within ~2%) to show E_w varies <1% — a clean way to demonstrate model stability without ground truth for the unknown words
Cross-model corroboration: Model II says EERIE is hard (E_w=709.9) and Model III independently classifies EERIE as 'hard' — the two results agree, used as a confidence argument
Standard classifier evaluation suite: precision/recall/F1/accuracy, confusion matrix, and one-vs-rest ROC curves, with honest reading that the 'hard' class is the weak spot
why_it_won: "It is a complete, internally consistent, and honestly-audited solution. Every required sub-task is answered with a concrete number and a named method, and the methods are chosen with justification rather than reflex — the log-then-MinMax normalization is motivated by a gamma-distribution argument, the feature set is pruned by RFE, and the small LSTM is deliberately small for the small sample. The standout judge-pleasing move is cross-validation between models: Task 2 (regression) and Task 3 (classification) independently agree that EERIE is hard, which the team explicitly uses as an evidence of confidence. Uncertainty is treated seriously and in multiple ways (150-run prediction interval, near-twin sensitivity analysis, sum-to-100% consistency check), which is exactly what Problem C judges reward in a 'data' problem. The Task-4 insight that human play does NOT track the decision-tree optimal depth (correlation 0.011 between depth and performance) is a genuine, non-obvious finding that shows the team thought about the data rather than just fitting it. Finally the letter is specific and actionable (difficulty-scored scoring + reward props to raise reporting rate), tying the modeling back to the editor's actual decisions."
section_highlights: "Strong structure: per-task chapters (Tasks 1-4) each following define-features → build-model → evaluate → apply-to-EERIE, so a reader can trace any required deliverable to one section. The Summary names all three models and their headline numbers up front (prediction interval, R²=0.68, accuracy 0.6556) — judges see the whole solution in one paragraph. Assumptions and a Notations table precede the models. Normalization, feature selection, and uncertainty each get their own labeled subsection rather than being buried. Figures carry the argument: the 150-run prediction histogram (Fig 8a), the sensitivity table of near-twin words (Table 3), the sum-to-100% line chart (Fig 11), the confusion matrix + ROC (Fig 15), and the depth/charNum/weighted correlation heatmap (Fig 16). The Strengths/Weaknesses section is candid (admits the SVM is weak on 'hard', round-off breaks the 100% sum, holidays ignored). The closing letter restates results in plain language with a concrete product recommendation."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Recursive 60-step LSTM forecasting compounds error at each step, but the reported prediction interval is built only from run-to-run variance (150 retrainings), so it captures model-initialization randomness, NOT accumulated multi-step forecast error or structural/model uncertainty — the interval is likely over-confident

The number of reported results was clearly declining, yet the model/letter forecast (~21.8k) and the framing don't deeply model the downward trend or its drivers; the LSTM essentially extrapolates a noisy series

R²=0.68 for Model II and ~0.66 accuracy for Model III are mediocre; the team somewhat oversells these ('high accuracy', 'great deal of confidence') given a 3-class problem where the majority class is ~60% of data — baseline accuracy isn't reported, so 0.656 may be only modestly above majority-class guessing

Seven independent regressors don't enforce the simplex constraint (percentages summing to 100, all non-negative); they audit it post-hoc but never constrain it, so predicted distributions are only approximately valid

Difficulty labels are self-generated from the same try-distribution data (E_w thresholds 600/700), then features are used to predict those labels — the 'ground truth' is a deterministic function of the outcome being modeled, risking circularity, and the 600/700 cutoffs are eyeballed from the histogram

Word attributes were shown to have weak correlation with hard-mode ratio and with performance (depth corr 0.011), which undercuts the premise that the chosen features can strongly predict the distribution — the modest R² is consistent with this
Twitter self-reported data is biased (only players who report, no cheating assumed) and percentages are pre-rounded, both acknowledged but not corrected for
Small data (359 days / ~359 words) limits all three models and is repeatedly cited as the fallback explanation for counterintuitive results (e.g., no learning over time)
reusable_patterns: Multi-output distribution prediction = train one regressor per output bin, then audit the sum-to-1/100% as a free uncertainty check — reusable for any 'predict a distribution' Problem-C task

Generate a single scalar difficulty/severity score via a domain-justified unequally-weighted average of an outcome vector, then (a) threshold it into class labels for a classifier and (b) reuse it as a sanity cross-check on a separate regression model — gives you two models that can corroborate each other
Build a prediction interval for any ML point-forecast by retraining N times (here 150) and taking mean ± z·sd across runs — cheap, defensible, presentable as a histogram
Sensitivity analysis without ground truth: pick near-twin inputs whose features differ by <2% and show the output varies <1% to argue stability
Justify every preprocessing choice from the data's distribution (log-transform because gamma-like and Min-Max alone fails on near-constant-fraction changes) rather than applying transforms by default
Hand-build interpretable features from external corpora (word-frequency lists, dictionaries, prior optimal-strategy decision trees) then prune with RFE to a small dominant set
Cite literature to pre-justify model choice (LSTM beats ARIMA by 84-87% error reduction) so the judge accepts the method before seeing results
Test the obvious confounder explicitly with a quick OLS + F/t-tests (does the word affect hard-mode %?) and report the null result as a finding
Mine one counterintuitive, decision-relevant insight for the 'other findings' task (human play diverges from machine-optimal depth) instead of listing trivia
Close with a stakeholder letter that converts each model output into one concrete product recommendation (difficulty-weighted scoring + reward props to lift reporting rate)
